questions.a: Central Processing Unit - The primary component of a computer that performs most of the processing
Arithmetic Logic Unit - Performs arithmetic and logical operations
Random Access Memory - Volatile memory used for temporary data storage
Read Only Memory - Non-volatile memory that retains data when powered off
Fast memory that sits between main memory and CPU to speed up data access
A memory space accessible by multiple processes for communication
Communication between processes through sending and receiving messages
A unidirectional communication channel between processes
Pipes with a specific name that exist in the filesystem and allow unrelated processes to communicate
Endpoints for communication between processes over a network
Asynchronous notifications sent to a process to notify it of events
Synchronization tools used to control access to shared resources
Mutual Exclusion - A lock that allows only one thread to access a resource at a time
A high-level synchronization construct that manages access to shared resources
Procedure call where the executing code runs on another address space (often a remote server)
A situation where two or more processes are waiting indefinitely for each other to release resources
A behavior where the system's outcome depends on the sequence or timing of uncontrollable events
Coordination of concurrent processes to ensure correct results
A classic problem where producers create data and consumers use it, requiring synchronization
A synchronization issue where readers and writers must access shared data without conflicts
A message queue used for communication between processes
A queue that holds events for a program to process asynchronously
A synchronization primitive that allows threads to wait for certain conditions to be met
An instance of a running program
A type of lightweight process that does not get its own address space.
The process of storing and restoring the state of a CPU so that multiple processes can share a single CPU
A data structure maintained by the OS to keep track of all processes
A data structure that stores information about a specific process
A data structure that stores information about a specific thread
A system call to create a new process by duplicating an existing one
A system call to replace the current process image with a new process image
A process that creates one or more child processes
A process created by another (parent) process
A process that has completed but still has an entry in the process table
A child process whose parent has terminated
Running multiple threads within a single process
Running multiple processes seemingly simultaneously
Dividing CPU time into slices for multiple processes
The part of the OS that decides which process runs next
The OS forcibly removes a running process to run another
A scheduling algorithm where each process gets an equal share of CPU time
Scheduling processes based on priority
A process that must complete within strict timing constraints
An abstraction that provides the illusion of a very large memory
The actual RAM installed in the computer
Dividing memory into fixed-size pages to manage memory efficiently
Dividing memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions
Hardware that handles virtual to physical address translation
An event that occurs when a program accesses a page not currently in memory
A cache that stores recent address translations
A data structure used to store the mapping between virtual addresses and physical addresses
Moving entire processes between disk and memory
Excessive paging causing system slowdown
A memory region for function calls and local variables
A memory region for dynamically allocated memory
A fixed-size block of physical memory used in paging
Table used in segmentation to store segment information
The range of memory addresses that a process can use
A virtual address
The actual address in physical memory
Wasted memory caused by unused space between or within memory allocations
Techniques to prevent processes from interfering with each other's memory
Loading pages into memory only when they are needed
A way to organize and store files on storage devices
A simple file system used in older and simpler storage devices
A modern file system used by Windows with advanced features
A data structure used to represent a file in UNIX-based systems
Algorithms to decide the order of servicing disk I/O requests
Time taken by the disk arm to move to the track where data is stored
Delay before data transfer begins following an instruction
A device that stores data in fixed-size blocks
A device that handles data as a stream of bytes
A fast storage device using flash memory
A traditional storage device using spinning disks
Redundant Array of Independent Disks - A way to store data across multiple disks for redundancy/performance
An integer that uniquely identifies an open file in a process
Disk space used as an extension of RAM
The core of the OS managing system resources
A minimalist kernel architecture providing only essential services
A single large process running entirely in a single address space
A restricted mode where applications run
A privileged mode where the OS runs
Software that loads the OS when a computer starts
The first process started during booting to initialize the system
A programmatic way for a user program to request service from the OS
A background process that runs without user interaction
A signal to the CPU to indicate an event needs immediate attention
A software-generated interrupt caused by an error or a specific request
Software that controls a hardware device
Distributing work evenly across resources
Current status of a process (e.g., running, waiting)
Queue where processes wait for CPU time
Assigning available resources to tasks
Scheduling the process that arrives first

questions.q: Shortest Job Next (SJN) / Shortest Job First (SJF)
questions.a: Scheduling the process with the shortest execution time next
Preemptive version of SJF, selecting the process with the shortest remaining time
Assigns a fixed time unit per process in rotation
Scheduling based on priority, optionally preempting lower priority processes
Multiple queues with different priority levels
Processes are given lottery tickets for CPU time allocation
Schedules tasks based on earliest deadlines
Services the disk I/O request closest to the current head position
Moves head back and forth across the disc from end to end, servicing requests as it passes over where they are located.
Disk arm services requests in one direction and jumps back to start
SCAN but reverses direction immediately when no more requests exist in current direction
C-SCAN but only goes as far as the last request before jumping
SCAN that divides requests into sub-queues for better service
Modified SCAN using two queues to improve performance
Replaces the oldest page in memory
Replaces the page that has not been used for the longest time
Replaces the page used least often
Approximation of LRU using a circular buffer
A Clock variant giving pages a second chance before replacement
Improved Clock algorithm using reference and modification bits
Randomly replaces a page in memory when a page frame is needed
Replaces pages based on referenced and modified bits
Keeps track of page usage over time to approximate LRU
